people_management.definition: Linguistic signals that a SWE job posting includes formal people-management responsibilities: having direct reports, leading/supervising a team, conducting 1:1s and performance reviews, owning headcount/hiring decisions, and managing career development. Distinct from purely technical leadership (architecture ownership) or informal mentorship, though boundaries are fuzzy and flagged below.

core_concepts.name: Direct reports
core_concepts.definition: Explicit references to having subordinate engineers who report to this role.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Word-boundary fine. The phrasal forms ('reports directly to you') need multi-word matching, not just \breports\b which would false-positive on 'status reports' or 'bug reports'.

core_concepts.name: People management role
core_concepts.definition: Job-title- or role-level descriptors indicating the position is a manager of people, not an IC.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'EM role' should require the 'role' suffix or other context — bare 'EM' is too noisy. 'manager' alone is intentionally NOT included here; it lives under Supervisory verbs / Team leadership where it has firmer context.

core_concepts.name: Team leadership
core_concepts.definition: Leading or heading a team of engineers, including tech-lead/lead-engineer hybrid roles that imply some people responsibility.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'lead' MUST NOT be matched alone — it appears constantly as a verb ('lead the design', 'lead initiatives') without people-management implication. All variants here are multi-word. 'tech lead' is borderline (often IC-with-influence rather than people manager) but conventionally counted in this concept; flagged in notes.

core_concepts.name: Supervisory verbs
core_concepts.definition: Verbs and verb phrases denoting oversight, supervision, or directing of staff.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'manage' is excluded because 'manage projects', 'manage stakeholders', 'manage your time' dominate JD usage. Restrict to the multi-word forms where the object is clearly people. 'oversee' is acceptable bare — but watch for 'oversee the architecture' / 'oversee delivery' which is technical, not people, oversight; consider reviewing collocations on the 100-post sample.

core_concepts.name: 1:1 meetings
core_concepts.definition: Recurring one-on-one meetings between manager and report, a hallmark of people-management cadence.
core_concepts.regex_notes: '1:1' contains a colon which is non-word; default \b…\b will fail. Recommend matching '1:1' WITHOUT word boundaries on the colon side — use lookarounds: (?<!\d)1:1(?!\d) so it doesn't match '1:10' timestamps or '1:1 ratio'. Same caution for '1-on-1'. The bare numeric '1:1' will also hit 'load balancer 1:1 mapping' and 'aspect ratio 1:1' — see exclusions.

core_concepts.name: Performance management
core_concepts.definition: Conducting performance reviews, calibrations, ratings, and performance-improvement processes for staff.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'PIP' should NOT be matched (Python Package Installer collision). Require 'PIP process' / 'on a PIP' / 'PIP plan' as multi-word. 'performance' alone is excluded — it appears in 'performance optimization', 'system performance', 'high-performance computing' constantly. Always require the full bigram.

core_concepts.name: Headcount and hiring authority
core_concepts.definition: Owning headcount, hiring decisions, recruiting, and interview-loop ownership for the team.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'build the team' / 'grow the team' overlap with general team-building rhetoric in startup JDs that may not imply formal headcount authority — flag for spot-check. 'interview' alone is excluded as too generic.

core_concepts.name: Coaching, mentoring, and career development
core_concepts.definition: Career growth, coaching, mentoring, and professional development of subordinates — the developmental side of people management.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'coach' as a bare verb is fine but watch for noun-compound noise (rare in SWE JDs). 'mentor' is sometimes used by staff/principal IC roles WITHOUT formal management — this concept overlaps with informal-mentorship; treated here as people-mgmt-adjacent. 'IDP' bare is too short — require 'IDP process' or 'IDPs'.

core_concepts.name: Team-size signals
core_concepts.definition: Explicit references to the size of the team being managed — quantitative signal of management scope.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'team of' is high-recall but must be paired with a number or 'engineers' to be a real signal — recommend a downstream regex like 'team of \d+' or 'team of [a-z]+ engineers'. Treat raw 'team of' counts as a noisy upper bound and flag in calibration.

core_concepts.name: Termination, hiring/firing authority
core_concepts.definition: Explicit hire/fire authority or staffing decisions — strongest possible people-mgmt signal but rarely stated explicitly in JDs.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Very low base rate in US LinkedIn JDs — expect single-digit hits per 10K postings. Useful as a high-precision signal.

exclusions.reason: Bare 'lead' is overwhelmingly a verb meaning 'drive/spearhead' (e.g., 'lead the design', 'lead initiatives', 'lead efforts') with no people-management content; also 'lead' the metal, 'lead time', 'lead generation'.
exclusions.guard: Never match bare \blead\b. Always require multi-word forms ('team lead', 'tech lead', 'lead a team', 'lead engineer').

exclusions.reason: Dominated by 'manage projects/stakeholders/expectations/risk/dependencies/your time' which are NOT people-management.
exclusions.guard: Require object-level disambiguation: 'manage (the|a) team', 'manage engineers/developers/staff/people'. Do not match bare \bmanage\b.

exclusions.reason: 'manager' appears in 'product manager', 'project manager', 'package manager', 'configuration manager', 'session manager', 'context manager' (Python).
exclusions.guard: Require qualifier: 'engineering manager', 'people manager', 'line manager', 'hiring manager'. Do not match bare \bmanager\b.

exclusions.reason: '1:1' literal also appears in 'aspect ratio 1:1', 'NAT 1:1', 'load balancer 1:1 mapping', timestamps like '1:10'.
exclusions.guard: Use (?<!\d)1:1(?!\d) plus prefer collocation with 'meeting', 'with', 'weekly', 'cadence' for high precision; or accept some noise and rely on the compound-form variants ('one-on-one', 'one on one') as the cleaner signal.

exclusions.reason: 'PR' = pull request, public relations, press release, Puerto Rico in location text, also a date prefix in 'PR-1234'.
exclusions.guard: Don't include bare 'PR' in this vocabulary at all (it's not in our list, but the analyst should be aware nothing in this list expands to 'PR').

exclusions.reason: 'PIP' = Python's pip package installer, very common in SWE JDs.
exclusions.guard: Require 'PIP process', 'on a PIP', 'PIP plan', or full expansion 'performance improvement plan'.

exclusions.reason: Generic 'reports' = 'status reports', 'bug reports', 'analytics reports', 'reporting dashboards', 'reporting tools'.
exclusions.guard: Require multi-word forms with 'directly', 'to you', 'reporting line/structure/chain/hierarchy'. Never match bare \breport\b.

exclusions.reason: 'head' verb/noun appears in 'head of partnerships', 'header', 'headers', 'overhead', 'heads-up display'.
exclusions.guard: Match 'head of engineering', 'head the team', 'heads the team' as multi-word only.

exclusions.reason: Dominated by technical 'performance optimization', 'system performance', 'high-performance', 'performance tuning', 'performance metrics'.
exclusions.guard: Always require bigram with review/evaluation/appraisal/assessment/management/feedback/calibration/rating/improvement.

exclusions.reason: Two-letter 'EM' collides with 'EM dash', 'EMR', 'EMC', email-marketing acronyms, and is rarely written this way standalone in JDs.
exclusions.guard: Require 'EM role' or 'as an EM' multi-word form. Prefer the spelled-out 'engineering manager'.

exclusions.reason: 'IC' (individual contributor) is in this vocabulary's adjacent space but also collides with 'IC design' (integrated circuit), 'eIC', AWS 'IAM'.
exclusions.guard: Not actually included in this list (IC is the OPPOSITE of people-mgmt), but if used elsewhere, require 'IC role', 'IC track', 'as an IC'.

exclusions.reason: Mostly fine in SWE JDs but can appear as a noun in product names or unrelated.
exclusions.guard: Acceptable bare; spot-check on 100-post sample.

exclusions.reason: Hits 'part of a team of engineers' (IC contexts) as well as 'manage a team of engineers'.
exclusions.guard: Pair with 'manage/lead/oversee/head' verb in a 5-word window, or restrict to 'team of \d+' for headcount-quantification.

people_management.calibration_recommendations: Spot-check these on a 100-post sample before trusting longitudinal trends: (1) 'Team leadership' — 'tech lead' / 'lead engineer' often denote senior-IC influence rather than formal management, and the IC-vs-manager mix may itself be shifting 2024->2026 (the very thing we want to measure), so distinguish formal-mgmt-lead from tech-lead in calibration; (2) '1:1 meetings' — verify the colon/hyphen variants are matching cleanly and that '1:1' isn't picking up technical ratios; (3) 'Headcount and hiring authority' — 'build the team' / 'grow the team' overlap with generic startup boilerplate; (4) 'Supervisory verbs' — confirm 'oversee' isn't dominated by 'oversee the architecture/delivery/migration' which is technical leadership not people supervision; (5) 'Coaching, mentoring, and career development' — staff/principal IC roles often claim 'mentor junior engineers' without people-mgmt authority, so this concept may over-count if used as a sole indicator.
people_management.notes: Overlaps to coordinate with sibling vocabularies: (a) 'tech lead' / 'lead engineer' overlap with a Technical-leadership vocabulary if one exists — recommend they live there exclusively and we drop them here, OR be counted as a separate 'ambiguous-lead' bucket; current draft keeps them under Team leadership but flags it. (b) 'mentor/mentoring' overlaps with informal-mentorship vocab; current draft keeps it here as people-mgmt-adjacent. (c) Job-title parsing (Engineering Manager, Staff Engineer, etc.) is best done from the structured title field rather than description-keyword density — these markers measure JD-body language, which is a different signal. (d) The colon character in '1:1' breaks default \b…\b semantics; consumer should use the lookaround pattern recommended in regex_notes. (e) Recommend reporting per-1000-words density rather than per-posting hit rate, since posting length itself is shifting over the 2024-2026 window and would confound counts. (f) For longitudinal trend reliability, consider locking the keyword list at analysis start and treating any ad-hoc additions as a separate sensitivity check.

orchestration.definition: Markers indicating the senior-engineer archetype of orchestrating work — both classical task decomposition, architecture, and specification authorship, and the emerging practice of directing AI/agent systems through context engineering, agent harnesses, repository instructions, memory, and multi-agent coordination. Each concept captures a distinguishable facet of how a human breaks down, plans, and steers execution (human or machine) toward an outcome.

core_concepts.name: Specifications and Requirements Authoring
core_concepts.definition: Producing written specs, requirements, design docs, RFCs, or PRDs that define what is to be built before implementation begins.
core_concepts.regex_notes: RFC and PRD are short and ambiguous; require word boundaries and prefer all-caps to reduce noise. 'spec' alone risks matching 'specifically' — must be word-bounded.

core_concepts.name: Task Decomposition and Planning
core_concepts.definition: Breaking large, ambiguous problems into tractable pieces, sequencing work, and planning execution.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'roadmap' and 'roadmapping' overlap with PM language; keep but expect noise. 'scope' alone is too generic — require 'scoping work' / 'scope out' / 'scope of work' style phrases.

core_concepts.name: Software Architecture and System Design
core_concepts.definition: High-level design of systems, components, and interfaces — the classical staff/principal orchestration of structure.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'architect' is also a job title — risk of matching role names ('Solutions Architect') rather than activity. Recommend not subtracting these (they still indicate the orchestration archetype).

core_concepts.name: Workflow Design and Pipeline Orchestration
core_concepts.definition: Designing workflows, pipelines, and process flows — including data/ML pipelines, CI/CD orchestration, and workflow engines.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'orchestration' is the topic name and overlaps with multiple concepts; placed here for the workflow sense. 'pipeline' alone is heavy-traffic — keep but expect high counts.

core_concepts.name: Context Engineering and Prompt/Spec Authorship for Agents
core_concepts.definition: Designing the context window, prompts, and specifications that LLMs/agents operate against — the emerging skill of writing for machine consumers.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'prompt' alone may match 'prompt response' or 'prompt action' (timeliness sense). Require contexts like 'prompt engineering', 'system prompt', 'prompt design', or 'prompts' (plural) when ambiguous.

core_concepts.name: Agent Harnesses and Coding Agents
core_concepts.definition: Tools, harnesses, and frameworks that wrap LLMs into autonomous coding/work agents (Claude Code, Cursor agents, Devin, etc.).
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'Cursor' alone may match the database/UI cursor — guard with surrounding 'Cursor IDE', 'Cursor agent', or co-occurrence with other agent tools. 'Aider' is rare so safe.

core_concepts.name: Repository Instructions and Agent Configuration Files
core_concepts.definition: Files like CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursorrules that encode how an agent should behave inside a repo.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Filenames need literal matching with periods preserved; word-boundary alone won't separate 'CLAUDE.md' from prose. Recommend a tokenizer that treats 'CLAUDE.md' as a unit or use whole-string substring contains for filename markers.

core_concepts.name: Agent Memory and State Management
core_concepts.definition: Persistent memory, context retention, and state systems that let agents accumulate knowledge across turns/sessions.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'memory' alone is far too generic (RAM, memory leaks). Only count when paired ('agent memory', 'long-term memory', 'persistent memory', etc.) — bare 'memory' MUST be excluded.

core_concepts.name: Multi-Agent Coordination
core_concepts.definition: Designing systems where multiple agents collaborate, with role specialization, handoffs, and supervision.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'A2A' is a 3-char ambiguous abbrev — require all-caps with word boundaries and surrounding agent context to count.

core_concepts.name: AI Tool and Agent Evaluation/Steering
core_concepts.definition: Reviewing, evaluating, steering, and accepting/rejecting agent output — the human-in-the-loop orchestration layer over AI work.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'guardrails' overlaps with safety/compliance topics — accept overlap or move to a sibling vocab if double-counted.

exclusions.reason: Bare 'memory' overwhelmingly refers to RAM, memory leaks, memory-safe languages, memory profiling, etc. It will dominate the AI-memory signal if counted alone.
exclusions.guard: Only count when adjacent to 'agent', 'long-term', 'short-term', 'persistent', 'episodic', 'semantic', 'working', 'conversation', 'session', 'vector', 'scratchpad', or paired with LLM/agent context.

exclusions.reason: Matches database cursors, UI cursors, and pagination cursors.
exclusions.guard: Require 'Cursor IDE', 'Cursor editor', 'Cursor agent', 'Cursor Composer', or co-occurrence within ~50 chars of another AI-tool token (Copilot, Claude, GPT).

exclusions.reason: Substring match would catch 'specifically', 'specialty', 'specialized', 'speculation', 'aspect'.
exclusions.guard: Word boundaries on both sides; safer to require 'spec ' or 'specs ' or attach to qualifiers ('tech spec', 'design spec', 'product spec').

exclusions.reason: RFC also denotes IETF protocol documents (RFC 1918, RFC 2616) referenced in networking JDs, not internal design RFCs.
exclusions.guard: Optionally negative-guard if followed by a number (e.g., 'RFC 2616'). Otherwise accept noise — both senses signal seniority somewhat.

exclusions.reason: Could match 'PRDxxx' codes; also rare false positives.
exclusions.guard: Require all-caps with word boundaries.

exclusions.reason: ADR is also 'American Depositary Receipt' (finance) — irrelevant in SWE JDs but possible at fintechs.
exclusions.guard: Word boundary, all-caps; accept negligible noise.

exclusions.reason: Bare DAG could match acronyms in unrelated contexts.
exclusions.guard: Require all-caps and word boundaries; accept overlap with workflow/data topics.

exclusions.reason: Generic word; 'scope of work', 'in scope', 'out of scope' are vague.
exclusions.guard: Use only the explicit 'scoping' / 'scoping work' / 'scope out' phrases listed.

exclusions.reason: Used heavily for data pipelines, sales pipelines, CI pipelines — high baseline count.
exclusions.guard: Accept the noise but report concept density rather than absolute counts; consider sub-tagging the qualifier.

exclusions.reason: Frequent as a job title noun ('Solutions Architect') rather than verb.
exclusions.guard: No guard needed — both senses signal the orchestration archetype, but track verb forms ('architecting', 'architected') separately if disambiguation matters.

exclusions.reason: Used generically for any business process; will inflate the workflow concept.
exclusions.guard: Accept; pair with 'workflow design', 'workflow orchestration', 'workflow engine' for tighter signal.

exclusions.reason: Could be Application-to-Application or unrelated abbreviation.
exclusions.guard: Require all-caps with word boundaries and co-occurrence with 'agent'.

exclusions.reason: Generic English word ('prompt response', 'prompt action').
exclusions.guard: Only count compound forms: 'prompt engineering', 'prompt design', 'system prompt', 'prompts' (plural), 'prompting'.

orchestration.calibration_recommendations: Highest-noise concepts: Workflow Design and Pipeline Orchestration (pipeline/workflow are generic), Software Architecture and System Design (architect-as-title vs activity), and Specifications/Requirements Authoring (spec/RFC/PRD ambiguity). The novel-AI concepts (Repo Instructions, Context Engineering, Agent Memory, Multi-Agent Coordination, Agent Harnesses) will be very sparse in 2024 and grow dramatically by 2026 — this is the strongest temporal signal in the topic and should be reported separately from the classical-orchestration concepts. Run separate calibration on Memory (huge baseline) and on agent-tool brand names (network effects: a JD listing one tool tends to list several).
orchestration.notes: Overlaps to coordinate with sibling vocabularies: (1) 'AI Tool and Agent Evaluation/Steering' overlaps with any 'AI Adoption' or 'Code Review' vocab — pick one home. (2) 'Workflow Design and Pipeline Orchestration' overlaps with any 'Data Engineering / MLOps' vocab — the tools (Airflow, Dagster, Temporal) belong in both contextually; recommend keeping here for the orchestration archetype but expect double-attribution. (3) 'guardrails' overlaps with AI safety/responsible-AI vocab. (4) 'roadmap'/'roadmapping' overlaps with product management vocab. (5) Repository instructions filenames (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md) require substring match rather than word-boundary tokenization because of the period. (6) The 'Architecture' concept intentionally subsumes both classical and AI-system architecture; if you want to split AI-system-architecture out, do so as a sub-axis rather than a separate concept here.

verification.definition: Language in job descriptions that signals the expectation of verifying that engineering work is correct, safe, and trustworthy before and after deployment. Covers classical software testing/QA, CI/CD-driven quality gates, code review as a correctness-checking activity, static analysis and compliance, reproducibility and artifact-based proof (logs, screenshots, recordings), evaluation harnesses for AI output, and post-deployment observability used to confirm behavior in production.

core_concepts.name: Automated testing
core_concepts.definition: Writing and maintaining automated tests to verify code behavior at unit, integration, end-to-end, or regression levels.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Use \b word boundaries; require 'test' to be embedded in a multi-token phrase or followed by a testing-context noun to avoid 'beta tester role', 'A/B test'.

core_concepts.name: QA and validation
core_concepts.definition: Quality-assurance language and validation/verification of correctness, including manual verification.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'QA' is acceptable as an uppercase token; 'QC' is risky (matches 'Quebec'), require quality-context co-token or full 'quality control'.

core_concepts.name: Evaluations and AI output verification
core_concepts.definition: Newer ML/AI-era vocabulary for evaluating model outputs, benchmarking, and human-in-the-loop verification of AI-generated work.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'eval' / 'evals' frequently mean 'evaluation period' in benefits sections; require nearby AI/ML/test/model token, or only count 'evals'/'eval harness'/'eval suite'.

core_concepts.name: CI/CD and quality gates
core_concepts.definition: Continuous integration / continuous delivery / deployment pipelines and the automated checks they enforce as gates on shipping code.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'CI' is high false-positive (Cincinnati, Cote d'Ivoire). Require uppercase token AND adjacent 'CD'|'pipeline'|'build'|'deploy'|'continuous'. 'CD' alone is also risky (compact disc, candidate-data); only count when paired with 'CI'.

core_concepts.name: Code review
core_concepts.definition: Peer review of code changes framed as a verification/correctness activity (catching defects, enforcing standards) rather than primarily as mentorship.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Adjacent topic 'Mentorship' also includes 'code review' framed as teaching; here we keep code review and tag overlap. Decision: count code review under Verification by default; in role-level analysis, treat 'mentor*' co-occurrence as mentorship-flavored.

core_concepts.name: Static analysis and code quality tooling
core_concepts.definition: Linters, type checkers, formatters, SAST/DAST scanners, and other automated correctness/quality tooling.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'Sonar' alone is risky (sonar tech); prefer 'SonarQube' or 'Sonar Cloud'. 'lint' OK with word boundaries.

core_concepts.name: Reproducibility and artifact proof
core_concepts.definition: Producing reproducible results and artifacts (logs, screenshots, recordings, traces) as evidence that work behaves as claimed.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'log' is high-frequency; only count compound forms ('logging', 'audit log', 'log evidence', 'observability log'), not bare 'log'. 'reproduce' near HR-context 'reproduce in employment' is rare in tech JDs but possible — require dev-context co-token.

core_concepts.name: Compliance and security verification
core_concepts.definition: Verification driven by external compliance regimes, security audits, and regulatory standards.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'audit' is general English; require compliance/security/log co-token or count compound forms. 'PCI' alone risks 'PCI bus' / hardware contexts; require 'DSS' or 'compliance'/'payment' co-token.

core_concepts.name: Post-deployment observability
core_concepts.definition: Production observability — monitoring, alerting, dashboards, SLO/SLA tracking — used to verify that deployed systems behave correctly.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'monitor' is broad; prefer 'monitoring' or compound 'production monitoring'. Bare 'SLA' / 'SLO' as uppercase tokens are usually safe in tech JDs.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'beta tester role', 'tested for drugs', 'A/B test' (marketing), 'testimonial', 'protest'.
exclusions.guard: Require multi-token compound ('unit test', 'test plan', 'integration test') or co-occurrence with 'code', 'unit', 'integration', 'regression', 'pipeline'. Block matches inside 'testimonial', 'protest', 'attest'.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'Cincinnati', 'Cote d'Ivoire (CI)', and stray two-letter tokens; also French 'ci'.
exclusions.guard: Only count uppercase 'CI' when adjacent to '/', '-', ' CD', 'pipeline', 'build', 'continuous'. Otherwise require full 'continuous integration'.

exclusions.reason: Compact disc, 'cd' shell command in unrelated context, 'candidate (cd.)'.
exclusions.guard: Only count when paired with CI ('CI/CD', 'CI-CD', 'CICD') or with 'continuous'.

exclusions.reason: Mostly clean in tech JDs but can match 'Q&A session', 'Doha (Qatar QA)'.
exclusions.guard: Block when followed by '&A', 'session', or when part of an acronym list of countries.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'evaluation period for benefits', 'performance evaluation' (HR review), 'evaluate candidates'.
exclusions.guard: Require AI/ML/model/test co-token within +/- 8 tokens; or only count 'evals', 'eval harness', 'eval suite', 'eval framework'. Exclude 'benefits', 'performance review', 'salary' co-tokens.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'data validation' (good) but also 'validation of identity', 'background validation', 'requires validation of right to work'.
exclusions.guard: Block within 5 tokens of 'identity', 'right to work', 'employment eligibility', 'background'.

exclusions.reason: Matches financial audit, internal audit (HR/finance), 'audited by E&Y'.
exclusions.guard: Require security/compliance/log/code co-token, or count only compound forms ('audit log', 'security audit', 'compliance audit', 'audit trail').

exclusions.reason: Matches 'monitor team performance' (mgmt), 'monitor budgets', 'heart monitor' (medical roles).
exclusions.guard: Prefer 'monitoring' / 'production monitoring' / co-tokens with 'metrics', 'alert', 'dashboard'.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'log hours', 'log into the system', 'travel log', 'blog'.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'logging', 'audit log', 'log evidence', 'log aggregation', or co-occurrence with 'metrics'/'trace'/'observability'.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'performance review' (HR), 'salary review', 'annual review' — strongly HR-flavored.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'code review', 'PR review', 'design review', 'peer review', 'security review' as multi-token phrases.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'EEO compliance', 'tax compliance', 'employment compliance' (HR boilerplate).
exclusions.guard: Block within 6 tokens of 'EEO', 'equal opportunity', 'employment', 'tax', 'I-9', 'background check'.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'PCI bus' / 'PCIe' in hardware/embedded JDs.
exclusions.guard: Require 'DSS' or 'compliance'/'payment' co-token; block when adjacent to 'bus', 'express', 'slot', 'lane'.

exclusions.reason: Mostly safe; can match 'Bratislava (SLA airport code)' rarely.
exclusions.guard: Word boundary + uppercase usually sufficient.

exclusions.reason: Naval/automotive sonar tech.
exclusions.guard: Prefer 'SonarQube', 'SonarCloud', 'Sonar scanner'.

exclusions.reason: Generally safe but can appear in 'lint roller' (rare in tech).
exclusions.guard: Word boundary; in tech JDs essentially clean.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'trace amounts', 'no trace of' in non-tech roles.
exclusions.guard: Require dev/observability co-token ('distributed', 'request', 'stack', 'span', 'OpenTelemetry').

verification.calibration_recommendations: After first pass, sample 100 random matches per concept stratified by year and role family. Specifically validate: (1) bare 'CI' / 'CD' / 'eval' / 'audit' / 'monitor' / 'review' / 'log' false-positive rates; if any exceeds ~5% drop the bare form and keep only compound forms. (2) Cross-tab 'code review' matches with the Mentorship topic — report co-occurrence rate and decide assignment policy at analysis time rather than at extraction time (i.e., extract once, label twice). (3) Spot-check 'evaluation' in benefits/HR sections: if >10% are HR-flavored, drop bare 'evaluation' and keep only 'eval', 'evals', 'eval harness', 'evaluation framework', 'evaluation pipeline', 'model evaluation', 'LLM evaluation'. (4) Confirm tooling brand names (Datadog, Sentry, etc.) are mentioned with verification intent and not just in a generic tech-stack list — if needed, count tooling separately as a distinct sub-feature.
verification.notes: Code-review overlap with Mentorship is intentional and flagged here per the prompt. Recommendation: do not double-count at extraction time; instead, store a per-mention 'topics' multilabel and resolve at the analysis layer using context (mentorship-context tokens like 'mentor', 'coach', 'grow junior engineers' nearby tip toward Mentorship; defect/bug/quality tokens tip toward Verification). The 'Evaluations and AI output verification' concept is the key 2024->2026 shift signal — evals/HITL/red-teaming/LLM-as-a-judge vocabulary should be tracked as its own sub-index in addition to rolling up to Verification, since that is a hypothesized growth area in the AI transition.

mentorship.definition: Language indicating that a senior engineer is expected to teach, grow, coach, and shepherd other engineers through influence rather than formal authority. Captures pair programming, code-reading and review-as-teaching, onboarding, knowledge transfer, architecture guidance, and explicit learning culture. Distinct from People-management (formal reports, perf reviews, hiring/firing) and Verification (review/testing as correctness gate).

core_concepts.name: Direct mentorship language
core_concepts.definition: Explicit use of mentor/mentee/mentorship terminology describing the role's responsibility to develop other engineers.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Match \bmentor\w*\b broadly; this concept is the strongest signal so generous matching is acceptable.

core_concepts.name: Coaching and developing engineers
core_concepts.definition: Coach/grow/develop/uplift terminology directed at engineers (not at customers, sales, leadership, or self).
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'coach'/'coaching' is risky — see exclusions. Prefer matching as multiword phrases that target engineers/team.

core_concepts.name: Teaching and knowledge transfer
core_concepts.definition: Teaching, instructing, knowledge sharing, brown-bags, tech talks, lunch-and-learns directed at fellow engineers.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Match 'teach\w*' but exclude 'teach yourself' / self-teaching. Avoid matching 'machine teaching' or 'teaching hospital' etc.

core_concepts.name: Guidance and influence
core_concepts.definition: Guiding, advising, supporting, leading-by-influence (not headcount). Captures the senior IC pattern of shaping work without direct reports.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'Guide' and 'lead' are heavily polluted by other senses — see exclusions. Strongly prefer multiword matches like 'guide the team', 'lead by example', 'technical guidance'.

core_concepts.name: Pair / mob / ensemble programming
core_concepts.definition: Explicit collaborative coding practices where teaching and learning happen synchronously between engineers.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'pairing' alone can match 'device pairing'/'wine pairing'; prefer compound forms or proximity to 'program/code/engineer'.

core_concepts.name: Onboarding and ramp-up
core_concepts.definition: Helping new hires become productive: onboarding, ramp-up, buddy systems, getting-started programs.
keywords: bring new engineers up to speed
get new hires up to speed
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'Onboarding' can refer to customer or user onboarding — see exclusions; require proximity to engineer/hire/team.

core_concepts.name: Code review as teaching
core_concepts.definition: Code review framed as a teaching/quality-uplift practice rather than purely a correctness gate. Note: overlaps with Verification; place here when paired with feedback/learning/growth language.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'review' is heavily overloaded (perf review, security review, business review). Anchor to 'code', 'design', 'architecture', 'PR', 'RFC', 'peer'. Note overlap with Verification — count here only when teaching/feedback framing is present.

core_concepts.name: Debugging and code-reading as a craft
core_concepts.definition: Reading and debugging unfamiliar code as a learning/teaching activity (often in mentorship/onboarding context).
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'debugging' is a core engineering skill and not inherently mentorship — count only when paired with collaborative or teaching language ('debug together', 'pair debug', 'walk through', 'walkthrough').

core_concepts.name: Architecture and design guidance
core_concepts.definition: Senior IC responsibility to shape technical decisions for and with other engineers (not just produce them solo).
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'Best practices' and 'standards' alone are weak signals; prefer compound forms with 'establish/define/drive/promote/share'.

core_concepts.name: Explicit learning culture
core_concepts.definition: Statements positioning the team/company/role as a place that prioritizes learning, growth, curiosity, continuous improvement of engineers.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'Career development' / 'professional development' often appear in benefits sections — context-bound but typically still indicate the company's stated stance toward engineer growth.

core_concepts.name: Communication and feedback skills
core_concepts.definition: Explicit expectation that the engineer gives/receives feedback well and communicates with engineers in ways that grow them.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'Multiplier' alone can match unrelated tech contexts; require proximity to team/engineers/people.

exclusions.pattern: user guide / style guide / installation guide / api guide / quick start guide
exclusions.reason: 'guide' as a noun referring to documentation, not a person guiding engineers.
exclusions.guard: Match 'guide(s|d|ing)' only as a verb directed at people, or require proximity (≤4 tokens) to engineers/team/peers/junior/others; exclude when preceded by 'user', 'style', 'install*', 'API', 'quick start', 'getting started' (unless 'getting started' is paired with onboarding context).

exclusions.pattern: coaching benefits / executive coaching / health coaching / wellness coach
exclusions.reason: 'coach/coaching' often appears in benefits sections referring to perks, not engineer-on-engineer coaching.
exclusions.guard: Exclude when within 5 tokens of 'benefit', 'perk', 'wellness', 'health', 'fitness', 'executive', 'life coach', 'career coach' (the last is ambiguous — keep if context is clearly internal mentorship).

exclusions.pattern: lead engineer / lead developer / leads the team / leads project
exclusions.reason: 'Lead' is overloaded: title noun, project lead, sales lead, lead generation. Only 'lead by example' / 'lead through influence' / 'tech lead' clearly indicate mentorship.
exclusions.guard: Match only specific phrases: 'lead by example', 'leading by example', 'lead through influence', 'leadership through influence', 'tech lead', 'technical lead'. Do not count bare 'lead'/'leads'/'leading'.

exclusions.pattern: performance review / business review / security review / compliance review
exclusions.reason: 'Review' alone covers many non-mentorship activities including formal perf reviews (which belong to People-management).
exclusions.guard: Anchor 'review' to 'code', 'design', 'architecture', 'architectural', 'arch', 'PR', 'pull request', 'RFC', 'spec', 'peer'. Exclude 'performance review', 'perf review', 'business review', 'compliance review', 'security review' (latter belongs to Verification).

exclusions.pattern: customer onboarding / user onboarding / client onboarding
exclusions.reason: 'Onboarding' frequently refers to customer/user flows, especially in product/growth roles.
exclusions.guard: Exclude when within 5 tokens of 'customer', 'user', 'client', 'merchant', 'partner', 'vendor', 'patient'. Keep only when paired with 'engineer', 'developer', 'hire', 'team', 'employee', 'new joiner'.

exclusions.reason: 'Ramp up' has a generic business meaning (scale up production/sales).
exclusions.guard: Require proximity to 'engineer', 'developer', 'new hire', 'team', 'newcomer', or to 'onboarding' within ±10 tokens.

exclusions.pattern: machine teaching / teaching hospital / teaching assistant
exclusions.reason: 'Teach/teaching' has unrelated technical and academic senses.
exclusions.guard: Exclude bigrams 'machine teaching', 'teaching hospital', 'teaching assistant'. For 'teach yourself', exclude as it indicates self-learning, not mentorship of others.

exclusions.pattern: device pairing / wine pairing / data pairing
exclusions.reason: 'Pairing' is used in non-programming contexts.
exclusions.guard: For 'pair'/'pairing' alone, require proximity (±4 tokens) to 'program', 'programming', 'code', 'coding', 'engineer', 'developer', 'work', 'session'.

exclusions.reason: 'Advocate' and 'champion' have non-mentorship business senses.
exclusions.guard: Require object to be 'engineers', 'team', 'best practices', 'quality', 'engineering culture', 'juniors', 'peers'.

exclusions.pattern: raise the bar (hiring / Amazon bar raiser)
exclusions.reason: 'Raise the bar' is a generic management phrase that can mean hiring standards or product quality, not necessarily mentorship.
exclusions.guard: Keep as a weak signal; in cells where co-occurrence with other Tier-1 mentorship terms is required, double-counting risk is low.

exclusions.reason: These are perks, not mentorship duties.
exclusions.guard: Exclude when in benefits/perks section header, or within 8 tokens of 'access to', 'free', 'subsidized', 'reimbursement'.

exclusions.pattern: L&D (learning and development as HR program)
exclusions.reason: 'L&D' may refer to corporate HR rather than the engineering role's mentorship duties — but for this study, company stance toward engineer growth is in scope.
exclusions.guard: Keep, but flag as 'culture/benefit' sub-signal vs. 'role responsibility' sub-signal in downstream analysis.

exclusions.pattern: raise the bar / set the bar (overloaded with Verification/quality)
exclusions.reason: Quality bar phrasing overlaps with Verification topic.
exclusions.guard: When co-occurring with 'engineering', 'team', 'others', count here; when co-occurring with 'product', 'customer', 'security', defer to that topic.

mentorship.calibration_recommendations: 1) Sample 200 postings stratified by year (2024/2025/2026) and seniority (junior/mid/senior/staff+); hand-label as mentorship-positive/negative. Compute precision/recall per concept and per keyword to identify weak terms. 2) Pay special attention to 'guide', 'coach', 'lead', 'review', 'onboarding', 'pairing', 'champion' — each has a high false-positive base rate. Implement the proximity guards in `exclusions` before counting. 3) Compute baseline density at junior level: mentorship markers should be near-zero in IC-junior postings and rise sharply at senior/staff/principal. If baseline is non-trivial, guards are too loose. 4) Consider a two-pass approach: Pass 1 unrestricted match -> Pass 2 apply exclusion guards. Report both for sensitivity analysis. 5) Track Tier-1 'Direct mentorship language' (mentor*) as the gold-signal anchor; other concepts should correlate with it within senior cells. If they do not, re-examine guards. 6) For overlap with People-management and Verification, build a co-occurrence matrix and report disjoint vs joint counts so the reader can see how decisions about boundary terms (code review, raise the bar) affect headline numbers.
mentorship.notes: Boundary calls: (a) 'Code review' — listed here because the brief includes 'code reading' and the dominant senior-IC framing is teaching; cross-listed with Verification with explicit guidance to count by surrounding feedback/learning vocabulary. (b) 'Architecture review' — included here per the brief; in flat-org / staff+ JDs this is the canonical mentorship-via-influence activity. (c) 'Tech lead' / 'technical leadership' — kept here (influence, not headcount); People-management list should keep 'engineering manager', 'people manager', 'direct reports', 'manage a team of N'. (d) 'Coaching' — strong false-positive risk from benefits language; require object-targeting at engineers/team. (e) Track 'mentor*' separately as the cleanest Tier-1 anchor for trend reporting. (f) For the 2024->2026 AI-transition story, expect 'pair programming'/'mob programming' density to potentially decline (LLM-as-pair-partner substitution) while 'code reading'/'code review'/'architecture review' may rise (humans verify and teach over machine output) — pre-register these as hypotheses so the vocabulary choice is not post-hoc. (g) British/American: 'mentor*', 'coach*', 'review*', 'onboard*', 'program(me)' — include 'programme'/'programmes' variants where compound forms occur ('mentorship programme', 'onboarding programme'); add to the variant list before final run.

performance.definition: Captures JD language demanding low-level performance work (profiling, latency/throughput tuning, memory/CPU optimization), systems-internals depth (OS, kernel, networking, distributed-systems internals, hardware-aware programming), and explicit depth-claim framings ('deep understanding of X', 'expert-level', 'from first principles'). Distinct from high-level architecture/orchestration: this is the internals/low-level end where employers demand expertise that AI tooling cannot easily substitute.

core_concepts.name: performance_optimization_general
core_concepts.definition: General references to performance work, tuning, and optimization as an explicit job activity.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Use word boundaries on 'perf' to avoid 'perfect/perform/perfume'; require it to appear in compound forms like 'perf tuning', 'perf work', 'perf engineering' or as standalone token followed by domain word.

core_concepts.name: profiling_and_benchmarking
core_concepts.definition: Mention of profilers, benchmarking, tracing, and instrumentation used to diagnose performance issues.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'profile' alone is ambiguous (LinkedIn profile, user profile) — only count compound forms or '-ing/-er' suffixes in tech contexts.

core_concepts.name: latency_throughput_scale
core_concepts.definition: Explicit metrics/objectives around latency, throughput, QPS, p99, and high-scale traffic.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'real time' is broad — keep but flag overlap with streaming/data-engineering vocab.

core_concepts.name: low_level_systems_programming
core_concepts.definition: Low-level programming work: assembly, C/C++ systems, memory management, SIMD/vectorization, lock-free data structures.

core_concepts.name: os_kernel_internals
core_concepts.definition: Deep operating-system and kernel internals knowledge.

core_concepts.name: networking_internals
core_concepts.definition: Deep networking-stack knowledge: TCP/IP internals, kernel bypass, RDMA, congestion control.

core_concepts.name: concurrency_parallelism
core_concepts.definition: Concurrency, parallelism, and multi-threading at a depth beyond standard async/await usage.

core_concepts.name: distributed_systems_internals
core_concepts.definition: Internals-level distributed systems work: consensus, replication protocols, consistency models, partitioning.

core_concepts.name: hardware_aware_programming
core_concepts.definition: Hardware-aware programming: GPU/CUDA, FPGAs, CPU microarchitecture, accelerator-specific optimization.

core_concepts.name: compilers_and_runtimes
core_concepts.definition: Compiler internals, JITs, garbage collection, runtime tuning.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'GC' alone is ambiguous (could mean General Counsel, Google Cloud) — require GC tuning/pauses/collector context or co-occurrence with JVM/heap.

core_concepts.name: database_storage_internals
core_concepts.definition: Database engine internals, query optimization, storage engine work.

core_concepts.name: depth_claim_language
core_concepts.definition: Explicit demand-for-depth phrasings employers use to signal expert-level / first-principles understanding.
core_concepts.regex_notes: These are template phrases — recommend pairing with a technical noun within ~5 tokens (e.g., 'deep understanding of distributed systems') to avoid generic 'deep understanding of our customers'. Capture both the phrase and a windowed-cooccurrence variant if possible.

core_concepts.name: scaling_and_efficiency
core_concepts.definition: Scaling-axis work and efficiency framings: scaling to N, cost/efficiency, resource utilization.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'scalability' is broad — keep, but note this concept overlaps with orchestration/architecture topic; treat overlap as additive rather than miscategorization.

core_concepts.name: algorithmic_optimization
core_concepts.definition: Algorithm- and data-structure-level optimization, complexity analysis.

exclusions.reason: HR/people-management term, not technical performance.
exclusions.guard: Exclude when followed by 'review/reviews/cycle/feedback/management'.

exclusions.reason: HR term for managing employee performance.
exclusions.guard: Exclude unless context contains technical terms (latency, throughput, system, code, application).

exclusions.reason: Fluff/culture phrase, not technical performance.
exclusions.guard: Exclude 'high[- ]performing team(s)' and 'high performance team(s)' / 'high-performance team(s)'.

exclusions.reason: HR/culture phrase.
exclusions.guard: Exclude 'high[- ]performance (culture|environment|workplace|organization)'.

exclusions.reason: Business KPIs, not system perf.
exclusions.guard: Exclude 'KPI', 'KPIs', 'key performance indicator(s)'.

exclusions.reason: Business metric, not engineering perf.
exclusions.guard: Exclude when 'performance' is preceded by sales/marketing/financial/business.

exclusions.reason: Compensation language.
exclusions.guard: Exclude 'performance (bonus|incentive|pay|based pay|based compensation)'.

exclusions.reason: 'profile' as social-media noun, not profiling.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'profile/profiler/profiling' in compound technical contexts (CPU/memory/heap/code/perf).

exclusions.pattern: user profile / customer profile / company profile
exclusions.reason: Generic noun usage of 'profile'.
exclusions.guard: Exclude when 'profile' is preceded by user/customer/company/candidate/applicant/buyer.

exclusions.reason: HR fluff using 'scale' metaphorically.
exclusions.guard: Exclude 'scale (your|our) (career|impact|skills|business)' unless followed by technical noun.

exclusions.pattern: deep understanding of our customers / business / market
exclusions.reason: Business empathy, not technical depth.
exclusions.guard: For depth_claim_language, require a co-occurring technical noun within 5 tokens (system, kernel, distributed, OS, hardware, GPU, networking, database, compiler, algorithm, etc.). Otherwise drop or label as non-technical-depth.

exclusions.reason: Generic discovery/analysis phrase, not depth claim.
exclusions.guard: Exclude standalone 'deep dive(s)/deep diving' unless followed by a technical noun.

exclusions.pattern: real estate / real time pay / real-time chat
exclusions.reason: 'real time' overlap with non-perf domains.
exclusions.guard: For 'real time/realtime', prefer co-occurrence with system/processing/streaming/data/inference/serving.

exclusions.reason: Rare false positives.
exclusions.guard: Word-boundary match; case sensitive optional.

exclusions.reason: Acronym collision.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'GC' in proximity to 'tuning/pause/heap/JVM/garbage/collector'.

exclusions.reason: Acronym collision (e.g., 'OS' could be other things).
exclusions.guard: Require 'OS internals/OS-level/operating system internals' rather than bare 'OS'.

exclusions.pattern: SME (subject matter expert) vs SME (small and medium enterprise)
exclusions.reason: Acronym collision in business contexts.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'SME' when co-occurring with technical domain noun, or prefer the spelled-out 'subject matter expert'.

exclusions.reason: Mostly safe; rare collision.
exclusions.guard: Generally accept; flag only if co-occurring with non-technical context.

exclusions.reason: 'Raft' is also a generic noun (e.g., 'a raft of features').
exclusions.guard: Require co-occurrence with consensus/replication/leader/distributed within ~10 tokens.

exclusions.pattern: Triton (NVIDIA GPU lang) vs Triton (Inference Server) vs Triton (other product names)
exclusions.reason: Multiple Triton products; mostly all relevant to perf.
exclusions.guard: Generally accept; both senses indicate hardware/perf depth.

exclusions.reason: Could be generic.
exclusions.guard: Treat 'fundamentals' as depth signal only when paired with technical domain noun (CS, OS, networking, distributed systems, etc.).

performance.calibration_recommendations: 1) Run a sample (n=200) of high-match postings and manually classify each as technical-perf vs HR-perf vs business-perf; the depth_claim_language and performance_optimization_general concepts will need the most filtering. 2) Implement co-occurrence guards for ambiguous tokens ('deep understanding of', 'real time', 'profile', 'GC', 'OS', 'scale') by requiring a technical noun within a 5-10 token window. 3) Treat depth_claim_language as a *modifier* concept: report it both raw and as 'depth_claim near technical noun' to separate fluff from genuine depth demand. 4) Track per-concept density (occurrences / 1000 tokens) rather than binary presence; this concept area will show most signal in the *intensity* dimension over 2024-2026. 5) Cross-check overlap with the Orchestration vocabulary: 'distributed systems' appears in both — decide whether to dual-count or assign primary topic by adjacent tokens (internals/consensus/replication => this list; architecture/design/microservices => Orchestration). 6) Validate hardware-aware terms (CUDA, FPGA, GPU) against role-title metadata — they spike in ML/infra roles and may need normalization across role cells.
performance.notes: This list emphasizes the *internals / low-level* end of the SWE skill spectrum, where the hypothesis is that AI substitution is weakest and JD demand may rise during 2024-2026. Three concepts (performance_optimization_general, depth_claim_language, scaling_and_efficiency) have the highest false-positive risk and should be reported with both raw counts and guarded counts. Compound forms ('performance-critical', 'low-latency') are listed both hyphenated and unhyphenated to handle inconsistent JD typography. Acronym-heavy concepts (CUDA, RDMA, DPDK, eBPF) are reliable signals — case-insensitive match recommended but consider preserving case-sensitive match for short acronyms that collide (BPF, GC, OS, SME). Depth-claim phrases are template-like; a windowed co-occurrence regex ('(deep|strong|expert|in-depth|thorough)\W+(understanding|knowledge|expertise|grasp|familiarity)\W+of\W+(\w+\W+){0,5}(<technical noun>)') will outperform raw-phrase matching.

process_scaffolding.definition: Vocabulary capturing traditional software-process, SDLC, and project-management language used in job descriptions: agile/scrum/kanban/waterfall methodologies, sprint and ceremony cadence, requirements engineering, specification authoring, V&V, project coordination, scheduling, milestones, change management, and governance. This is the classical process-and-methodology layer that orchestrates human teams building software, distinct from agentic-AI task orchestration and from correctness-gating verification.

core_concepts.name: Agile methodology
core_concepts.definition: Generic agile framing language: agile principles, agile delivery, agile mindset, agile transformation, agile coaching.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Use \bagile\b to avoid 'fragile'/'fragility'. Treat 'agile/scrum' and 'agile-scrum' as the same token — strip punctuation before matching.

core_concepts.name: Scrum framework
core_concepts.definition: Scrum-specific roles, ceremonies, and artifacts.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'SM' and 'PO' are dangerous abbreviations; only count when adjacent to scrum-context tokens or expansion already matched. 'Scrum' alone is safe (no common collision).

core_concepts.name: Sprints and iterations
core_concepts.definition: Time-boxed iteration vocabulary and story-point estimation.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'sprint' is largely safe in JD context; avoid only if a fitness/athletics context is suspected (rare in SWE JDs).

core_concepts.name: Kanban / Lean / Waterfall and other methodologies
core_concepts.definition: Non-scrum process frameworks frequently named alongside agile.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'lean' alone is fine; 'XP' is risky (Windows XP, experience points) — require nearby 'extreme programming' or 'agile'/'scrum' context. 'V-model' and 'V model' need word boundaries to avoid 'V model number'.

core_concepts.name: Requirements engineering
core_concepts.definition: Eliciting, analysing, and documenting product/system requirements.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Match 'requirement(s)?' as base form. 'NFR' is safe in technical context; 'DoD' could collide with 'Department of Defense' — require nearby 'definition'/'done' or other agile token.

core_concepts.name: Specification authoring
core_concepts.definition: Writing technical, functional, design, or product specs.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'spec' alone is risky (could be 'spectrum', 'special', 'specific'); require word boundary and avoid prefix matches. Use \bspec(s|ification|ifications|ifying|ify|ced|ed)?\b. 'RFC' could match RFC HTTP standards — usually still process-context-relevant.

core_concepts.name: Verification & Validation (V&V)
core_concepts.definition: Process-level verification and validation activities (distinct from low-level test verification).
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'V&V' should be matched literal (with optional spaces around &). Standalone 'verification'/'validation' will overlap with the Verification topic — recommend reserving the V&V acronym + 'verification and validation' phrasing for this concept and giving the bare verbs to the Verification topic.

core_concepts.name: SDLC and process governance
core_concepts.definition: Lifecycle, governance, change-management, and process-improvement vocabulary.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'CR' is too generic — only count if other change-management tokens nearby. 'audit'/'audits' might also appear in security context; tolerate overlap.

core_concepts.name: Project coordination
core_concepts.definition: Coordinating people, work, and dependencies across teams.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'orchestrate' overlaps with the Orchestration topic — restrict to 'orchestrate teams'/'orchestrating teams' here; leave bare 'orchestrate' to the agentic-AI topic.

core_concepts.name: Scheduling and milestones
core_concepts.definition: Time-boxed planning, milestones, deadlines, roadmaps, and delivery commitments.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'schedule' is broad — include only when in project/delivery context if precision is critical, otherwise tolerate.

core_concepts.name: Project / program management roles & tooling
core_concepts.definition: PM roles, certifications, and ticket-tracking tools that index process scaffolding.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'PM' has many false positives ('a.m./p.m.', 'private message', 'particulate matter', 'PMP'); guard with surrounding tokens. 'Linear' is risky (linear regression / linear algebra) — only count when surrounded by tooling context (Jira, Asana, etc.) or capitalised in product-name form.

exclusions.reason: Substring of 'fragile', 'fragility', 'agileness'.
exclusions.guard: Use word boundaries: \bagile\b (case-insensitive). Do NOT match inside other words.

exclusions.reason: Generally safe; rarely collides with non-process terms.
exclusions.guard: Word boundaries sufficient.

exclusions.reason: Collides with 'a.m./p.m.' (post meridiem), 'private message', 'particulate matter', PM2.5, 'project memo'.
exclusions.guard: Require uppercase match AND adjacency to one of: 'project', 'program', 'manager', 'PMP', 'PMO', 'TPM', 'PMI', 'PMBOK'. Exclude when preceded/followed by digit-colon time pattern (e.g., '5 PM', '12:00 PM').

exclusions.reason: Collides with 'purchase order', 'PO box', 'P.O.'.
exclusions.guard: Only count when adjacent to 'product owner', 'scrum', 'agile', 'CSPO', 'PSPO'. Do not match 'P.O.'.

exclusions.reason: Collides with social media, single-mode, 'small', or initials.
exclusions.guard: Only count when adjacent to 'scrum', 'master', 'CSM', 'PSM'.

exclusions.reason: Could match 'Department of Defense' (frequent in US gov JDs).
exclusions.guard: Only count if accompanied by 'definition of done' OR 'agile/scrum' tokens within the same sentence.

exclusions.reason: Collides with 'Windows XP', 'XP-Pen', 'XP value/experience points'.
exclusions.guard: Require 'extreme programming' expansion OR co-occurrence with 'agile'/'scrum'/'pair programming'.

exclusions.reason: Substring of 'special', 'specific', 'specialty', 'spectrum', 'speculation'.
exclusions.guard: Word boundary required: \bspec(s|ification|ifications|ifying|ify|ced|ed|'d)?\b. Reject 'special*', 'specific*' explicitly.

exclusions.reason: Collides with 'lean toward', 'lean on', 'leaning'.
exclusions.guard: Match with adjacency: 'lean methodology', 'lean software', 'lean principles', 'lean startup', or co-occurrence with 'agile'.

exclusions.reason: Heavy overlap with the separate Verification topic in the paper.
exclusions.guard: Reserve the bare verbs/nouns for the Verification topic; here count ONLY the explicit 'V&V', 'IV&V', 'verification and validation', 'validation and verification' phrasings.

exclusions.reason: Common general-English verb/noun.
exclusions.guard: Acceptable; tolerate as polysemous. If precision is critical, restrict to 'project schedule', 'delivery schedule', 'release schedule', 'on schedule', 'schedule risk'.

exclusions.reason: Also appears in 'coordinate system', 'coordinate plane', 'GPS coordinates'.
exclusions.guard: Reject if followed by 'system', 'plane', 'axis', 'geometry'. Otherwise count.

exclusions.reason: Belongs primarily to the Orchestration (agentic AI) topic.
exclusions.guard: Here count only the bigrams 'orchestrate teams' / 'orchestrating teams'; let the Orchestration topic claim bare 'orchestrate'.

exclusions.reason: Collides with 'linear regression', 'linear algebra', 'linear model'.
exclusions.guard: Only count when in a tooling list (near 'Jira', 'Asana', 'ticket', 'issue tracker'); require capital L.

exclusions.reason: Also matches HTTP/IETF RFCs.
exclusions.guard: Acceptable overlap — engineering RFC culture is in scope for process scaffolding.

exclusions.reason: Could match 'art', 'artwork', 'artist'.
exclusions.guard: Only count when uppercase ART AND co-occurring with 'release train', 'SAFe', 'PI planning'.

exclusions.reason: Too generic.
exclusions.guard: Only count if 'change request' is also present in the document, or CR is adjacent to 'CAB'/'change'.

exclusions.reason: Could match 'retrofit', 'retroactive', 'retrograde'.
exclusions.guard: Word boundary required; or require co-occurrence with 'sprint'/'scrum'.

process_scaffolding.calibration_recommendations: 1) Run a 200-posting random-sample audit per quarter and flag postings where ambiguous tokens (PM, PO, SM, DoD, XP, spec, lean, retro) fire without their disambiguating context — measure precision per token; drop or re-guard tokens scoring <0.85 precision. 2) Build a co-occurrence guard: count an ambiguous abbreviation only if at least one unambiguous process-scaffolding token (e.g., agile, scrum, sprint, kanban, jira) appears in the same posting. 3) Pre-strip punctuation around '/' '&' '-' so 'agile/scrum', 'agile-scrum', 'V&V', 'V & V', 'change-management' all normalise. 4) Lowercase before matching except for acronyms PM/PO/SM/DoD/XP/ART/CR/PMP/PMO/TPM/SDLC/CMMI/ITIL/SAFe — keep these case-sensitive to reduce false positives. 5) Track per-concept density (occurrences / token-count) AND per-posting binary presence; the binary measure is more robust to JD length variation. 6) Consider weighting Tier 1 concept counts equally rather than summing all variants — it neutralises vocabulary-bloat where 'scrum'-heavy JDs dominate just because they list every ceremony.
process_scaffolding.notes: Boundary calls: (a) bare 'verification'/'validation' is delegated to the separate Verification topic; this topic claims only the V&V/IV&V acronym and the explicit 'verification and validation' phrasing — flag this in the paper's methods section so the two topics don't double-count. (b) 'orchestrate' similarly delegated to the Orchestration topic except for explicit 'orchestrate teams'. (c) PM/program-management tooling (Jira, Asana, etc.) is included because it is a strong proxy for ticket-driven process scaffolding; if the paper prefers tooling to live in a separate 'tooling' topic, lift the eleventh concept out. (d) DevOps/CI-CD vocabulary is intentionally NOT included here — that belongs to a deployment-pipeline topic. (e) The paper's hypothesis (that this layer shrinks relative to orchestration as senior work shifts to agentic AI) means false negatives are costlier than false positives at the senior tier — recommend the looser Tier-2 keyword set for senior-cell measurements and the stricter guarded set for cross-cohort comparisons.

legacy_stack.definition: Technologies, platforms, and stacks that signal a legacy, pre-cloud-native, pre-LLM enterprise environment in software engineering job descriptions. These include mainframe languages, legacy Microsoft and Java EE stacks, deprecated database platforms, on-prem virtualization/identity tooling, legacy enterprise integration buses, and legacy desktop/client frameworks. The presence of these markers in a JD suggests maintenance of pre-2015-era enterprise systems rather than greenfield cloud-native or AI-era development.

core_concepts.name: Mainframe languages and platforms
core_concepts.definition: Languages and operating environments tied to IBM mainframe and midrange systems, typically used for legacy financial, insurance, and government workloads.
core_concepts.regex_notes: RPG must be guarded against role-playing-game and 'RPG' in gaming contexts. Use a co-mention rule (RPG within 100 chars of AS/400, IBM i, COBOL, iSeries, etc.). 'IBM i' is short and risky — require word boundary and 'IBM' prefix.

core_concepts.name: Legacy general-purpose languages
core_concepts.definition: Older general-purpose languages whose presence in a modern JD typically implies maintenance of legacy code rather than new development.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Pascal is high-noise (matches names, 'Pascal case'); require co-mention with Delphi or 'Object Pascal'. VBA is moderately current in finance/Excel contexts — treat as ambiguous. Perl alone is borderline; only flag 'Perl 5' or co-mention with mainframe markers.

core_concepts.name: Legacy Microsoft .NET stack
core_concepts.definition: Pre-.NET Core era Microsoft web and application frameworks tied to Windows Server / IIS hosting and the .NET Framework 4.x runtime.
core_concepts.regex_notes: CRITICAL: '.NET' alone matches modern .NET 6/7/8 — DO NOT count bare '.NET' as legacy. Only count '.NET Framework' (with 'Framework' qualifier) or specific legacy sub-products. '.NET' requires custom regex because '.' is non-word boundary; use pattern like (?<![\w.])\.NET\b. ASP.NET MVC (without version) is borderline — only MVC 5 / older is clearly legacy.

core_concepts.name: Legacy Microsoft server / collaboration
core_concepts.definition: On-prem Microsoft server products and collaboration suites that predate the cloud-first (Azure/M365) era.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'SharePoint' and 'Exchange' are too ambiguous (SharePoint Online is current; 'Exchange' is a common English word). Require version qualifier or 'on-prem'/'on premise' modifier. VSS is highly ambiguous (Volume Shadow Copy, vector space, etc.) — only count when near 'Visual SourceSafe' or other legacy MS markers.

core_concepts.name: Legacy Java EE / app server stack
core_concepts.definition: Pre-Spring-Boot, pre-microservices Java enterprise application servers and EE specifications associated with monolithic deployments.
core_concepts.regex_notes: WAS is highly ambiguous (English verb); only count when near 'WebSphere' or 'IBM'. JSP and Servlets are borderline — still appear in current JDs but increasingly legacy; flag as soft signal. 'Java 7' and earlier is clearly legacy; Java 8 is borderline (still widely supported).

core_concepts.name: Legacy enterprise SOAP / web services
core_concepts.definition: SOAP-based web service standards and WS-* protocols associated with pre-REST enterprise integration.
core_concepts.regex_notes: SOAP alone is borderline (still used in some integrations); strongest signal is co-mention with WSDL or 'SOAP web services'. XSD is too generic (XML schemas are current) — drop or require co-mention with SOAP.

core_concepts.name: Legacy enterprise applications and ERPs
core_concepts.definition: On-prem ERP, CRM, and groupware suites that predate the SaaS / cloud-CRM era and typically require specialized legacy maintenance.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'Domino' alone matches Domino's Pizza and DominoDB; require 'Lotus' or 'IBM' prefix or co-mention. 'World' (JDE World) is too generic on its own — require co-mention with JD Edwards. SAP S/4HANA is current; only legacy when 'R/3' or 'ECC' qualifier present. ABAP is still active but is a strong legacy-Microsoft-style signal.

core_concepts.name: Legacy database platforms
core_concepts.definition: Database engines and platforms associated with pre-cloud, on-prem deployments — particularly older mainframe and proprietary relational systems.
core_concepts.regex_notes: DB2, Teradata, Sybase, Informix are still in active use but are strong legacy-stack signals — count them. PL/SQL and T-SQL alone are NOT legacy (current); only count specific old SQL Server / Oracle versions. Oracle generic is current — only old version qualifiers count as legacy.

core_concepts.name: Legacy virtualization and on-prem infrastructure
core_concepts.definition: On-prem hypervisors, virtualization suites, and remote-desktop platforms that predate or compete with public-cloud-native infrastructure.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'Citrix' on its own is the company name; specifically 'Citrix XenApp/XenDesktop/Workspace' indicates the virtualization product. ESX matches in technical contexts but also possibly in chemistry abbreviations — keep word boundary. VMS alone is risky (matches 'CMS', 'LMS', etc.); prefer 'OpenVMS'.

core_concepts.name: Legacy identity and directory services
core_concepts.definition: On-prem identity, authentication, and directory services that predate cloud-native IAM (Okta, Azure AD/Entra, AWS IAM).
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'AD' alone is extremely high noise (Anno Domini, advertising, 'ad-hoc', 'add'); ALWAYS require 'Active Directory' spelled out, or 'AD' co-mentioned within 50 chars of 'LDAP', 'GPO', 'domain controller', or 'Kerberos'. LDAP and Kerberos are still in current use but are legacy-leaning signals. 'Azure AD' / 'Entra ID' are MODERN — exclude these from the pattern.

core_concepts.name: Legacy enterprise integration / ESB / messaging
core_concepts.definition: Heavyweight enterprise service bus and message-broker products associated with pre-event-streaming (Kafka) integration patterns.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'MQ' alone matches MQ-9 Reaper, McQuarrie, and other unrelated abbreviations — require 'IBM MQ', 'WebSphere MQ', or 'MQSeries' (or co-mention with IBM/messaging). MuleSoft is borderline-legacy (still actively sold but signals integration-heavy enterprise environment). SOA is borderline — pattern is older but term still used.

core_concepts.name: Legacy version control and build
core_concepts.definition: Pre-Git version control systems and pre-CI/CD build tools indicating a legacy SDLC.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Perforce is still actively used in gaming/large monorepos — borderline legacy. CVS is highly ambiguous (CVS pharmacy, curriculum vitae); require 'Concurrent Versions' or co-mention with version control terms. Make / Ant are technically legacy build tools but extremely common — flag as soft signal. Hudson is the pre-fork name of Jenkins; clearly legacy.

exclusions.reason: Bare '.NET' matches BOTH legacy (.NET Framework 4.x) and modern (.NET 6/7/8, .NET Core). Modern .NET is current and dominant in 2024-2026 JDs.
exclusions.guard: Only count '.NET Framework' (with the word 'Framework') or specific legacy sub-products (WebForms, WCF, VB.NET, Classic ASP). Exclude '.NET Core', '.NET 5', '.NET 6', '.NET 7', '.NET 8', 'ASP.NET Core'. Use lookahead/lookbehind: \.NET(?!\s*(Core|[5-9]|\d{2})).

exclusions.reason: Two-letter token matches Anno Domini, advertising ('ad'), 'ad-hoc', 'add', and many other words.
exclusions.guard: ALWAYS require 'Active Directory' spelled in full, OR 'AD' within 50 characters of 'LDAP', 'GPO', 'domain controller', 'Kerberos', or 'forest/domain'. Never count bare 'AD'.

exclusions.reason: Azure AD (now Microsoft Entra ID) is a MODERN cloud identity service, not legacy on-prem AD.
exclusions.guard: Explicitly exclude 'Azure AD', 'AAD', 'Entra ID', 'Entra', 'Microsoft Entra' from the Active Directory legacy match.

exclusions.reason: Matches 'MQ-9 Reaper' (drone), McQuarrie, and various abbreviations.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'IBM MQ', 'WebSphere MQ', 'MQSeries', or 'MQ' co-mentioned within 50 chars of 'queue', 'broker', 'JMS', or 'IBM'.

exclusions.reason: 'Citrix' is the company name; only the virtualization products (XenApp, XenDesktop, Workspace, Virtual Apps and Desktops, Citrix Receiver) are the legacy-virt signal. Citrix Networks / Citrix ADC is a different product line.
exclusions.guard: Prefer 'Citrix XenApp', 'Citrix XenDesktop', 'Citrix Virtual Apps', 'Citrix Workspace'. Bare 'Citrix' counts but with lower weight.

exclusions.reason: Highly ambiguous: Volume Shadow Copy Service, Vector Space Search, various other meanings.
exclusions.guard: Only count when explicitly 'Visual SourceSafe' is spelled out, or 'VSS' co-mentioned with 'Visual SourceSafe', 'source control', or 'version control'.

exclusions.reason: Matches role-playing-game contexts, especially in gaming/entertainment JDs.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'RPG' when co-mentioned with 'AS/400', 'IBM i', 'iSeries', 'COBOL', 'mainframe', or as 'RPGLE'/'RPG IV'/'RPG/400'.

exclusions.reason: Common English past-tense verb 'was'.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'WAS' when co-mentioned with 'WebSphere' within 50 chars, or replace with 'WebSphere Application Server'.

exclusions.reason: CVS pharmacy is a major US employer; also matches 'curriculum vitae'.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'CVS' when co-mentioned with 'version control', 'Subversion', 'Git', 'source control', or spelled out as 'Concurrent Versions System'.

exclusions.reason: Matches Domino's Pizza, Project Domino, DominoDB (a different product), Domino Data Lab (modern ML platform).
exclusions.guard: Only count 'Domino' when co-mentioned with 'Lotus', 'IBM', or 'Notes'. Exclude 'Domino Data Lab'.

exclusions.reason: Matches the personal name Pascal, 'Pascal case' naming convention, and Blaise Pascal references.
exclusions.guard: Only count 'Pascal' when 'Object Pascal', 'Delphi', or 'Turbo Pascal' is co-mentioned. Exclude 'PascalCase'/'Pascal case'/'PascalCasing'.

exclusions.reason: Three-letter abbreviation has other meanings (e.g., 'Earnings Before...', 'Energy...').
exclusions.guard: Only count 'ESB' when 'Enterprise Service Bus' is spelled out OR co-mentioned with TIBCO/Mule/BizTalk/SOA.

exclusions.reason: Java 8 is still widely supported (LTS) — borderline legacy. Including it would over-count.
exclusions.guard: Exclude Java 8 from legacy markers; only count Java 7 and earlier (or 'JDK 1.6'/'JDK 1.7').

exclusions.reason: Make is universal in C/C++ and Linux ecosystems; Ant is still used but legacy-leaning. Including would over-count.
exclusions.guard: Treat as 'soft' legacy signal only if other legacy markers co-occur in the same JD.

exclusions.reason: SOAP integrations exist in current JDs (especially financial/healthcare); not always legacy.
exclusions.guard: Strongest signal is 'SOAP' + 'WSDL' co-mention or 'SOAP web services'. Treat bare 'SOAP' as soft signal.

legacy_stack.calibration_recommendations: 1) Compute per-keyword document frequency on a 1% sample of 2024 vs 2026 JDs and sanity-check the top 20 contributors per concept — drop or guard any keyword whose top-document examples are dominantly false positives. 2) For ambiguous tokens (.NET, AD, MQ, RPG, WAS, CVS, Domino, Pascal, VSS), apply co-mention guards described in `exclusions` and re-measure. 3) Build a 'legacy density score' as count of distinct concepts triggered (not raw keyword count) to dampen vocabulary-size bias across concepts. 4) Hold out a 100-JD calibration set, manually label as legacy/modern/mixed, and tune per-concept weights to maximize agreement with human labels (target Cohen's kappa >= 0.7). 5) Track 'modern .NET' (.NET Core, .NET 6/7/8, ASP.NET Core, Blazor, MAUI) as a SEPARATE concept so the legacy vs modern split inside the Microsoft ecosystem can be measured directly. 6) Run keyword density both as raw count per 1000 tokens and as binary presence per JD; report both in the paper because long JDs in regulated industries can inflate raw counts.
legacy_stack.notes: Java 8, Spring (non-Boot), JSP, Servlets, Maven, Perforce, MuleSoft, SOAP, ABAP, LDAP, Kerberos, Hyper-V are all 'borderline' — they appear in both legacy and modern JDs. We include them but recommend treating each as a soft signal that gains weight only when co-occurring with at least one strong signal (mainframe language, .NET Framework, EJB, Active Directory on-prem, BizTalk, etc.). The strongest single-keyword legacy signals are: COBOL, JCL, AS/400, iSeries, z/OS, VB6, Classic ASP, ASP.NET WebForms, .NET Framework, EJB 2, WebLogic, WebSphere, Lotus Notes, PeopleSoft, Siebel, BizTalk, ClearCase, Visual SourceSafe, ColdFusion, Delphi, PowerBuilder, OpenVMS, Hudson. The brief's example of bare '.NET' is INTENTIONALLY downgraded here — see exclusions — because conflating modern and legacy .NET would systematically bias the 2024-vs-2026 trend. Recommend the paper explicitly note this design choice.

context_infrastructure.definition: The informational substrate that enables effective engineering work: human- and machine-readable documentation, observability and telemetry, data-pipeline hygiene, product/business literacy, and cross-functional communication artifacts. Captures language about authoring and maintaining the context (docs, runbooks, dashboards, ADRs, data integrations, stakeholder alignment) on which engineering decisions depend, as opposed to the act of writing code itself.

core_concepts.name: Technical documentation authoring & maintenance
core_concepts.definition: Writing, updating, and curating engineering-facing docs that describe how systems work and why.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Require qualifier (technical/engineering/system/design/code/internal/developer/dev/wiki/readme/knowledge base) or a verb pairing (write/maintain/author/update/curate); bare 'documentation' or 'docs' is too generic.

core_concepts.name: Architecture decision records & RFCs
core_concepts.definition: Authoring or reviewing ADRs, RFCs, design proposals, and similar deliberative artifacts.
core_concepts.regex_notes: RFC alone is risky (IETF RFC NNNN cites). Match RFC only when adjacent to 'process', 'document', 'write', 'author', 'review', 'propose', or as 'RFCs' plural; avoid 'RFC \d+' patterns.

core_concepts.name: Runbooks, playbooks, & operational docs
core_concepts.definition: Step-by-step operational artifacts for incidents, on-call, and recurring procedures.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Strong signal terms; few false positives. SOP plural form helps disambiguate from unrelated acronyms.

core_concepts.name: Observability & telemetry stack
core_concepts.definition: Metrics, logs, traces, dashboards, and the systems that produce/consume them.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'monitor'/'monitoring' is broad — it sometimes means physical hardware monitor or generic supervision. Prefer pairings with logs/metrics/traces/alerts or with named tools. 'metric'/'metrics' alone is fine if observability context is plausible; KPI/business-metric guard handled in exclusions.

core_concepts.name: Service-level reliability targets
core_concepts.definition: Defining and tracking SLOs/SLAs/SLIs and reliability budgets that anchor operational quality.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Acronyms are clean signals in JD context. SLA can sometimes refer to vendor contracts; pairing with 'define', 'meet', 'set', 'monitor', 'track' tightens.

core_concepts.name: Data-pipeline & data-integration hygiene
core_concepts.definition: Maintaining well-integrated, well-documented data flows so downstream consumers and analyses are trustworthy.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'data pipeline' / 'data integration' / 'data quality' are strong. Bare 'data' is far too broad; require the compound.

core_concepts.name: System understanding & internal knowledge sharing
core_concepts.definition: Activities that build and propagate shared mental models of the system: diagrams, onboarding, knowledge transfer.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'C4' is short — only match 'C4 model' / 'C4 diagram'.

core_concepts.name: Product & business literacy
core_concepts.definition: Understanding of customers, product strategy, business metrics, and domain context that engineers translate into technical decisions.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'KPI' / 'OKR' are clean. 'business' / 'customer' / 'product' alone are too generic; require the compound forms listed.

core_concepts.name: Cross-functional communication & coordination
core_concepts.definition: Language about working across teams (PM, design, data, marketing, sales, support, legal) and translating between technical and non-technical audiences.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Bare 'stakeholder' / 'communicate' is fluff; only count when in compounds (stakeholder management/alignment/communication, communicate technical concepts, etc.).

core_concepts.name: Technical writing craft
core_concepts.definition: Explicit references to writing skill itself — clear written communication as a job skill, beyond generic 'communication'.
core_concepts.regex_notes: Distinct from broad 'communication' fluff. Require the writing/written modifier.

core_concepts.name: API & interface documentation
core_concepts.definition: Documenting public/internal APIs, schemas, and contracts so consumers (human or agent) can use them correctly.
core_concepts.regex_notes: 'DX' is short — match only when adjacent to 'developer experience'.

exclusions.reason: Bare 'documentation' is generic — appears in legal/HR/compliance contexts and as boilerplate ('documentation as needed').
exclusions.guard: Require a qualifier (technical/engineering/system/design/code/internal/developer/API/runbook/playbook) or a verb (write/author/maintain/update/curate) within ±3 tokens.

exclusions.reason: Often refers to Google Docs as a tool, or appears as 'docs and tickets' boilerplate.
exclusions.guard: Require qualifier (technical/engineering/system/design/API/dev/developer/internal) or pair with verb.

exclusions.reason: Pure fluff in JDs ('strong communication skills').
